subject: Computer Networking
title: Mainframes and Terminals
description: A micro-lesson that makes Mainframes and Terminals usable.

hook.text: In the 1960s, one giant computer served an entire building. Users shared it through dumb terminals on their desks.

buildup.text: Terminals had no processing power. They sent keystrokes to the mainframe and displayed whatever it sent back.

discovery.text: Time-sharing let dozens of users work on one mainframe simultaneously. Each got a slice of CPU time.

twist.text: Terminals connected via dedicated serial cables. Each new user meant another physical wire — expensive and rigid.

climax.text: This centralized model planted the seed: if terminals could connect over phone lines, distance wouldn't matter.

punchline.text: Mainframes proved sharing was possible. Networks removed the distance barrier.

quiz.question: What did time-sharing allow on mainframes?
options: Multiple users to share one computer simultaneously
